entries.title: Circumcision and Naming of Jesus
entries.blurb: The eighth day of Christmas: the child is circumcised and given the name Jesus — 'the LORD saves' — the name the angel gave before he was conceived.

entries.title: Luther excommunicated
entries.blurb: The bull Decet Romanum Pontificem formally excommunicates Martin Luther after he refused to recant — cutting the reformer loose from the Roman Church. See the vault's Protestant Reformation note.

entries.title: Epiphany — the Magi (and, in the East, the Baptism of Christ)
entries.blurb: The manifestation of Christ to the Gentiles in the visit of the Magi. Eastern churches keep this day as Theophany, commemorating Jesus' baptism in the Jordan.

entries.title: Antony of Egypt
entries.blurb: Commemoration of the father of Christian monasticism, who gave away his goods and withdrew to the Egyptian desert; his Life by Athanasius shaped the monastic movement.

entries.title: The Confession of Peter
entries.blurb: Marking Peter's great confession — 'You are the Christ, the Son of the living God.' The day opens the Week of Prayer for Christian Unity.

entries.title: The Conversion of Paul
entries.blurb: The risen Christ confronts Saul on the road to Damascus, turning the church's fiercest persecutor into its greatest missionary. The day closes the Week of Prayer for Christian Unity.

entries.title: Timothy and Titus
entries.blurb: Feast of the two young co-workers of Paul, entrusted with the churches of Ephesus and Crete, to whom three of his letters are addressed.

entries.title: Presentation of Jesus at the Temple (Candlemas)
entries.blurb: Forty days after the Nativity, Jesus is presented in the Temple, where Simeon greets him as 'a light for revelation to the Gentiles.'

entries.title: Cyril and Methodius, apostles to the Slavs
entries.blurb: The brother-missionaries who evangelised the Slavic peoples and devised the alphabet that carried Scripture and liturgy into their tongue.

entries.title: Edict of Thessalonica
entries.blurb: Emperor Theodosius I makes Nicene Christianity the official religion of the Roman Empire — a decisive turn from persecuted faith to established church.

entries.title: Patrick, missionary to Ireland
entries.blurb: Commemoration of the fifth-century bishop who, once a captive slave in Ireland, returned to evangelise the island. Traditionally kept on the day of his death.

entries.title: Joseph of Nazareth
entries.blurb: Feast of the carpenter of Nazareth, husband of Mary and guardian of the child Jesus — the 'just man' who obeyed the angel's word.

entries.title: The Annunciation
entries.blurb: Gabriel announces to Mary that she will bear the Son of God — nine months before Christmas. Her answer: 'Let it be to me according to your word.'

entries.title: The Azusa Street Revival begins
entries.blurb: In a Los Angeles mission on Azusa Street, a revival under William Seymour ignites in April 1906 — widely reckoned the birthplace of the worldwide Pentecostal movement.

entries.title: Luther before the Diet of Worms
entries.blurb: Ordered to recant, Luther refused unless convinced by Scripture and plain reason — the words later summarised as 'Here I stand; I can do no other.' See the vault's Protestant Reformation note.

entries.title: George, martyr
entries.blurb: Commemoration of the soldier-martyr of the Diocletian persecution, later patron of England and of many lands East and West.

entries.title: Mark the Evangelist
entries.blurb: Feast of the writer of the second Gospel, traditionally the interpreter of Peter and founder of the church in Alexandria.

entries.title: Philip and James, Apostles
entries.blurb: Joint feast of two of the Twelve — Philip, who brought Nathanael to Jesus, and James 'the Less.'

entries.title: First Council of Nicaea opens
entries.blurb: The first ecumenical council, convened by Constantine, meets to answer the Arian controversy and gives the church the original Nicene Creed. See the vault's Constantine and Athanasius of Alexandria notes.

entries.title: Wesley's Aldersgate experience
entries.blurb: At a meeting in Aldersgate Street, London, John Wesley felt his heart 'strangely warmed' and trusted Christ alone for salvation — a turning point for the Evangelical Revival.

entries.title: The Fall of Constantinople
entries.blurb: The Byzantine capital falls to the Ottoman Turks, ending the thousand-year Eastern Roman Empire and turning the great church of Hagia Sophia into a mosque — a watershed for Eastern Christianity.

entries.title: The Visitation
entries.blurb: Mary visits Elizabeth, who greets her as 'the mother of my Lord,' and Mary answers with the Magnificat: 'My soul magnifies the Lord.'

entries.title: Boniface, apostle to the Germans
entries.blurb: Commemoration of the English monk who evangelised the German lands — felling the pagan Oak of Thor — and was martyred at Dokkum with his companions.

entries.title: Barnabas the Apostle
entries.blurb: Feast of the 'son of encouragement,' the Levite who vouched for the converted Paul and travelled with him on the first missionary journey.

entries.title: Exsurge Domine threatens Luther
entries.blurb: Pope Leo X issues the bull Exsurge Domine, condemning 41 of Luther's propositions and giving him sixty days to recant — the formal opening of Rome's case against the Reformation.

entries.title: Council of Ephesus opens
entries.blurb: The third ecumenical council affirms that Mary bore one who is truly God (Theotokos) and condemns Nestorius — safeguarding the unity of Christ's person.

entries.title: Nativity of John the Baptist
entries.blurb: Kept six months before Christmas, marking the birth of the forerunner who would 'prepare the way of the Lord.'

entries.title: The Augsburg Confession presented
entries.blurb: The foundational Lutheran statement of faith is read before Emperor Charles V at the Diet of Augsburg — the confessional charter of the Reformation churches.

entries.title: Peter and Paul, Apostles
entries.blurb: Joint feast of the two great apostles, by tradition both martyred at Rome — Peter the apostle to the Jews, Paul to the Gentiles.

entries.title: Thomas the Apostle
entries.blurb: Feast of the apostle who doubted, then confessed the risen Christ as 'My Lord and my God,' and by tradition carried the gospel to India.

entries.title: Jan Hus burned at Constance
entries.blurb: The Czech reformer is burned at the Council of Constance despite a promised safe-conduct — a Protestant forerunner a century before Luther.

entries.title: Thomas More executed
entries.blurb: On the same date, 120 years after Hus, the Lord Chancellor Thomas More is beheaded for refusing to accept Henry VIII as head of the church in England — 'the king's good servant, but God's first.'

entries.title: Benedict of Nursia
entries.blurb: Commemoration of the father of Western monasticism, whose Rule — ordering a life of prayer and work, 'ora et labora' — shaped European Christianity for a millennium.

entries.title: The Great Schism (East–West)
entries.blurb: Legates of the pope and the patriarch of Constantinople exchange mutual excommunications, the symbolic break between the Roman Catholic and Eastern Orthodox churches.

entries.title: Mary Magdalene
entries.blurb: Feast of the first witness of the resurrection, called by the early church 'the apostle to the apostles.'

entries.title: James the Great, Apostle
entries.blurb: Feast of the son of Zebedee, one of the inner three, and the first apostle to be martyred.

entries.title: Mary, Martha and Lazarus of Bethany
entries.blurb: The household of Bethany whom Jesus loved — Martha who served, Mary who sat at his feet, and Lazarus whom he raised from the dead.

entries.title: The Transfiguration of Jesus
entries.blurb: On the mountain Jesus is transfigured before Peter, James and John, his face shining like the sun, with Moses and Elijah beside him and the Father's voice: 'This is my beloved Son.'

entries.title: Laurence, deacon and martyr
entries.blurb: Commemoration of the deacon of Rome who, ordered to hand over the church's treasure, presented the poor — and was martyred in the Valerian persecution.

entries.title: Dormition (Assumption) of Mary
entries.blurb: The oldest Marian feast, kept in the East as the Dormition ('falling asleep') and in the West as the Assumption — marking the end of the Virgin's earthly life. Not observed by most Protestant churches.

entries.title: Bartholomew the Apostle
entries.blurb: Feast of one of the Twelve, often identified with Nathanael, whom Jesus called 'an Israelite in whom there is no deceit.'

entries.title: St Bartholomew's Day Massacre
entries.blurb: A wave of mob and state violence against French Protestants (Huguenots) begins in Paris and spreads across France — thousands killed. A grievous day the feast now shares.

entries.title: The Beheading of John the Baptist
entries.blurb: The church remembers the forerunner's martyrdom — beheaded by Herod for rebuking his marriage, at the demand of Herodias' daughter.

entries.title: John Chrysostom
entries.blurb: Commemoration of the 'golden-mouthed' preacher and archbishop of Constantinople, greatest of the Greek Fathers as an expositor of Scripture, exiled for his plain rebukes of the powerful.

entries.title: Holy Cross Day (Exaltation of the Cross)
entries.blurb: A day to glory in the cross of Christ, kept since the fourth-century dedication of the church built over Golgotha in Jerusalem.

entries.title: Matthew, Apostle and Evangelist
entries.blurb: Feast of the tax-collector called from his booth to follow Jesus, and the writer of the first Gospel.

entries.title: Michael and All Angels (Michaelmas)
entries.blurb: A celebration of the angels and of Michael the archangel, 'protector of God's people' in Daniel and Revelation.

entries.title: Francis of Assisi
entries.blurb: Commemoration of the merchant's son who embraced poverty to follow Christ, founded the Franciscan order, and preached the gospel to rich, poor and creation alike.

entries.title: Council of Chalcedon opens
entries.blurb: The fourth ecumenical council defines that Christ is one person in two natures, fully God and fully man — the touchstone of orthodox Christology.

entries.title: Second Vatican Council opens
entries.blurb: Pope John XXIII opens the twenty-first ecumenical council, whose reforms — vernacular liturgy, ecumenical outreach — reshaped the modern Roman Catholic Church.

entries.title: Latimer and Ridley martyred at Oxford
entries.blurb: Bishops Hugh Latimer and Nicholas Ridley are burned under Mary I. Latimer's words to Ridley endured: a candle lit in England that would never be put out.

entries.title: Luke the Evangelist
entries.blurb: Feast of 'the beloved physician,' companion of Paul and author of both the third Gospel and the Acts of the Apostles.

entries.title: Simon and Jude, Apostles
entries.blurb: Joint feast of two of the Twelve — Simon the Zealot and Jude (Thaddaeus), traditionally paired in mission.

entries.title: Reformation Day — the Ninety-Five Theses
entries.blurb: By tradition Martin Luther posts his theses against the sale of indulgences on the Castle Church door in Wittenberg — the spark of the Protestant Reformation. See the vault's Protestant Reformation note.

entries.title: All Saints' Day
entries.blurb: A feast of the whole company of the redeemed — 'a great multitude that no one could number' — from every nation, tribe and tongue.

entries.title: All Souls' Day (Commemoration of the Faithful Departed)
entries.blurb: A day of remembrance and thanksgiving for all who have died in Christ.

entries.title: Martin of Tours
entries.blurb: Commemoration of the soldier who, converted, shared his cloak with a beggar and became bishop of Tours — one of the first non-martyrs the church honoured as a saint. His feast, Martinmas, long marked the start of winter.

entries.title: Andrew the Apostle
entries.blurb: Feast of the first-called disciple, who brought his brother Simon Peter to Jesus; patron of Scotland.

entries.title: Nicholas of Myra
entries.blurb: Commemoration of the fourth-century bishop famed for secret generosity — the historical root of Father Christmas — and, by tradition, a defender of Nicene orthodoxy.

entries.title: Luther burns the papal bull
entries.blurb: At Wittenberg's Elster Gate, Luther publicly burns the bull Exsurge Domine and volumes of canon law — a point of no return in the break with Rome.

entries.title: Council of Trent opens
entries.blurb: The Roman Catholic Church's answer to the Reformation begins its long series of sessions, defining doctrine and reforming abuses. See the vault's Council of Trent (1546) note.

entries.title: The Nativity of Jesus Christ (Christmas)
entries.blurb: The birth of the Saviour at Bethlehem — 'the Word became flesh and dwelt among us.' The central feast of the incarnation.

entries.title: Stephen, the First Martyr
entries.blurb: The day after Christmas the church remembers its first martyr, stoned while praying for his killers — 'Lord, do not hold this sin against them.'

entries.title: John, Apostle and Evangelist
entries.blurb: Feast of the beloved disciple, writer of the fourth Gospel, three letters, and the Revelation.

entries.title: The Holy Innocents
entries.blurb: The church remembers the children of Bethlehem killed by Herod in his attempt to destroy the newborn king — the first to die for Christ.

entries.title: Thomas Becket martyred at Canterbury
entries.blurb: The Archbishop of Canterbury is cut down in his own cathedral by knights of Henry II after a long struggle over the church's liberties — Europe's most famous medieval martyrdom.
